title: Examining Vietnam Gaming Market Opportunity 2026
purpose: Open the deck with the market question and geographic focus.
evidence: Deck title and brand framing only.
visual: Bold title slide with Garena-style red accent and Vietnam label.
takeaway: This deck tests whether Vietnam is a near-term sourcing opportunity in 2026.
notes: Use as the entry slide for HTML and PowerPoint.

title: Executive Summary
purpose: Start the summary section.
visual: Centered section-break title.
takeaway: Lead with the answer, then let the body prove it.

title: Macro & Cultural Factors
purpose: Start the readiness-factor section.
visual: Section break with subtitle for demand-side and supply-side drivers.
takeaway: Establish whether Vietnam is becoming more ready for local game success.

title: Income per Capita Trends & Regional Benchmarking
purpose: Show whether rising purchasing power is improving Vietnam's readiness for higher-value gaming spend, using actual World Bank GDP per capita data through 2024.
visual: Custom benchmark slide: 2015-2024 Vietnam trend bars on the left, KPI cards plus ASEAN benchmark CAGR bars on the right.
takeaway: Vietnam still trails richer ASEAN peers on absolute income, but its faster GDP-per-capita growth supports stronger medium-term upside for discretionary gaming spend.
notes: Source: World Bank.

title: Demand for Digital Entertainment
purpose: Use official sector-value data to infer long-run demand growth for digital entertainment in Vietnam, on a 2015-2024 window consistent with slide 8.
evidence:
National Statistics Office of Vietnam sector-value series, 2015-2024:
- Information & Communication (VND bn): 29,392 to 387,489
- Arts & Entertainment (VND bn): 24,969 to 71,488

2015-2024 CAGR:
- Information & Communication: 33.18%
- Arts & Entertainment: 12.40%
visual: Line chart with 2 lines: Information & Communication and Arts & Entertainment, plus two CAGR scorecards.
takeaway: The steady uptrend in demand for Information & Communication and Arts & Entertainment services probably implies a steady rise in demand for digital entertainment, including games.
notes: Source: National Statistics Office of Vietnam.

title: Demographics of Vietnamese Gamers
purpose: Use actual game-level demographics to show which age cohorts dominate the highest-grossing mobile games in Vietnam.
evidence:
Top 10 grossing games in Vietnam by 2025 revenue from the local warehouse, with age-bucket distributions pulled from Sensor Tower demographics and combined across app variants using average 2025 VN DAU as weights.

Important method note:
- Revenue ranking is Vietnam-only
- Demographics use Sensor Tower's SE_ASIA geography because VN is not supported directly by that endpoint
- App-level demographic mixes are combined using avg DAU in Vietnam during 2025
- Sensor Tower bucket labels are rendered here as age bands: 0-17, 18-24, 25-34, 35-44, 45-54
visual: Stacked horizontal bar chart by game using age bands 0-17, 18-24, 25-34, 35-44, and 45-54, plus a short portfolio read-through.
takeaway: Mobile gamers of biggest games in Vietnam are mostly made up of those who are under 25 years old, with the age group of 18-24 contributing the most players.
notes: Source: SensorTower (Revenue-adjusted for Vietnam market; Demographics of SE_ASIA is used as proxy for VN and app variants are combined using average 2025 VN DAU weights).

title: Vietnam Overall Demographics
purpose: Recreate Vietnam's changing population pyramid from 1950 to 2025 so we can visually explain how the country's age structure evolved into today's gaming-relevant demographic base.
evidence: Annual Vietnam age-by-sex population pyramid data for 1950-2025, including total population and median age. Local recreation based on PopulationPyramids.org and UN World Population Prospects 2024.
visual: Interactive animated population pyramid with play controls, year slider, and quick-jump buttons.
takeaway: Vietnam has a large under-25 population of 37.2 million, equal to 36.6% of the population, which serves as a major potential target audience for gaming.
notes: Source: PopulationPyramids.org / UN World Population Prospects 2024.

title: Under-25 Population Trend, 1980-2025
purpose: Show how Vietnam's under-25 audience base has evolved over time in both absolute size and share of population.
evidence: Vietnam under-25 population (0-24) from 1980 to 2025, using the same local population-pyramid dataset as slide 11. Bar = number of people. Line = under-25 share of total population.
visual: Combo chart with bars for under-25 people and line for under-25 percentage share, 1980-2025.
takeaway: With a rapidly aging population and low replacement rate, Vietnam's under-25 population has already been in a downtrend. If this trend continues, games with mostly youthful user bases will eventually face active-user pressure.
notes: Source: PopulationPyramids.org / UN World Population Prospects 2024.

title: Hardware
purpose: Show that device access and connectivity are already strong enough to support mass mobile play, meaningful PC play, and limited console play.
evidence:
Hardware and connectivity evidence:
- 100.7M smartphone subscribers in Feb 2024
- 127.6 mobile cellular subscriptions per 100 people in 2024
- 84.15% internet users in 2024
- 99% 4G population coverage in Nov 2024
- 90% 5G population coverage by Feb 2026
- 82.3% of households on fiber in Oct 2024
- 68% of urban 18-39 respondents owned a desktop or laptop in a 2017 Q&Me survey
- Console penetration remains below 5%
visual: Custom hardware-readiness slide with mobile access metrics on the left and PC / console context on the right.
takeaway: Vietnam is already a mobile-first hardware market: smartphone and internet access are effectively mass-market, urban PC ownership is still meaningful, and console remains niche.
notes: Sources: Freedom House, World Bank/TradingEconomics, Vietnam+, VietnamNet, VnExpress, Pew Research, Q&Me, Digital in Asia.

title: Vietnam Gaming History: Console Button Era
purpose: Show how gaming first took root in Vietnam through rented consoles, arcade corners, and neighborhood spectators before any formal industry existed.
evidence:
Era focus:
- 1990s
- Famicom / NES clones and PS1 as luxury devices
- Cartridge console shops and arcade centers
- Hyper-local, face-to-face gaming culture
visual: Large placeholder collage on the left, structured era panel on the right, and static four-era tabs on top.
takeaway: Gaming in Vietnam began as a local, face-to-face pastime built around rented consoles and shared neighborhood play.
notes: Source: The Evolution of Gaming in Vietnam research document.

title: Vietnam Gaming History: PC Gaming & Internet Boom
purpose: Show how ADSL, Quan Net culture, and the first publishers turned gaming into a mass youth habit while also triggering the strongest social backlash.
evidence:
Era focus:
- 2000-2010
- ADSL rollout and Quan Net dominance
- VNG, VTC Game, FPT Online
- LAN classics and first online-game boom
visual: Large placeholder collage on the left, structured era panel on the right, and static four-era tabs on top.
takeaway: The internet era made gaming mass-market in Vietnam, but it also produced the country's strongest stigma around games.
notes: Source: The Evolution of Gaming in Vietnam research document.

Era focus:
- 2011-2020
- 3G/4G and affordable smartphones
- Flappy Bird global shockwave
- Garena, streaming, and esports professionalization
visual: Large placeholder collage on the left, structured era panel on the right, and static four-era tabs on top.

title: Vietnam Gaming History: Digital Economy Era

Era focus:
- 2021-present

- Amanotes, iKame, Sky Mavis / Axie Infinity

- 2024: >2,000 bn VND from international users on Google Play (apps + games)

title: THE RISE OF MADE-IN-VIETNAM ENTERTAINMENT: MOVIES
purpose: Use Vietnam cinema output and box-office data to show that domestic entertainment production has expanded meaningfully over time, even while international films still dominate total releases.
evidence:
Movie counts use unique movie_slug by listing_year from the merged Moveek + Box Office Vietnam sheet, split between Vietnamese and non-Vietnamese titles. Top-grossing Vietnamese movies use boxofficevietnam_total_revenue_vnd where available.

Directional local counts:
- 2010: 4 Vietnamese vs 18 non-Vietnamese
- 2015: 40 vs 187
- 2020: 22 vs 168
- 2025: 46 vs 232
visual: Left: stacked yearly chart of Vietnamese and non-Vietnamese movie slugs. Right: top-grossing Vietnamese movie table with release year, title, and revenue.
takeaway: While international movies still take up the majority, the number of Vietnamese movies going to the cinema has trended upward over time, and we increasingly see more high-grossing local releases.
notes: Source: Moveek + Box Office Vietnam merged cinema dataset.

Directional counts used for the slide:

2026 is excluded as partial/in-progress coverage.
visual: Left: stacked bar chart of Vietnamese and non-Vietnamese concert counts by year. Right: hero image of Anh Trai Say Hi as an iconic local-concert example.

title: THE RISE OF MADE-IN-VIETNAM ENTERTAINMENT: YOUTUBE VIDEOS?
purpose: Use daily YouTube trending-language data to show whether Vietnamese-language content still dominates attention on a global platform, then ground it with one concrete day-level trending snapshot.
evidence:
Daily Vietnamese-video share uses the language-count sheet from 2022-07-01 to 2025-06-30. The right-side table uses unique trending videos captured on 2025-06-30 from the per-video sheet, deduplicated by video_id within the day.

Key stats:
- Average daily Vietnamese share: 70.79%
- 2025-06-30 Vietnamese share: 61.02%
- Unique trending videos on 2025-06-30: 59
visual: Left: dense daily line chart of Vietnamese-video share in YouTube trending. Right: scrollable unique trending videos table for 2025-06-30.
takeaway: While Vietnamese videos still make up the majority of YouTube trending in Vietnam, there is a gradual decline over time, showing that Vietnamese audiences are increasingly watching more YouTube content from international creators.
notes: Source: most_popular_vn_daily_video_language_counts.csv and most_popular_vn_with_is_vietnamese_video.csv.

title: SHIFTING GOVERNMENT STANCE: OVERVIEW
purpose: Open the supply section with a broad, two-dimensional view of Vietnam's gaming supply landscape — covering both production ecosystem scale/capability AND the policy environment that shapes who can operate. This slide frames the full supply story before slides 22-26 go into each dimension in detail.
evidence:
Draw from BOTH research docs. Structure the slide around two supply dimensions and one structural paradox.

Dimension 1 — Production ecosystem (from Vietnam Game Production Ecosystem doc):
Ecosystem scale (2025 KPIs):
- 210 active domestic studios, 2,679 registered publishers (26th globally, +27.39% YoY)
- 27,388 new game titles launched in 2025 (33rd globally in production volume)
- $1.66B total revenue (2025) -> $2.42B projected (2029), CAGR 9.77%
- 9,300 downloads per minute; 4.91B total downloads in 2025
Capability arc (very brief — slide 26 goes deep):
- 2000-2011: AAA art outsourcing foundation (Glass Egg: Call of Duty, Spider-Man; Gameloft: 1,200 staff)
- 2012-2019: Self-publishing mobile boom (Flappy Bird -> Amanotes 3B+ downloads)
- 2020-2026: Premium PC/console + Web3 emergence (Axie Infinity $4.1B peak; Hoa Webby Awards; Thần Trùng 91% on Steam)

Dimension 2 — Policy environment (from Vietnamese Game Development Government Support doc):
- Gaming officially designated one of Vietnam's 6 key cultural industries (2023)
- State directive: 'Do local, go global' — explicit ambition to build a global export hub
- Dual structure: generous fiscal incentives for local studios (10% CIT, VAT exemption, R&D 200% deduction) + strict compliance gate for operators (Decree 147, Dec 2024)
- Who consolidates: committed local operators (VNG, Garena) | Who exits: casual cross-border entrants (Riot, Blizzard)

The structural paradox (bottom callout):
- 94.47% of downloads are exports — only 5.53% domestic consumption
- Domestic market is tightly regulated; most studios build lightweight titles specifically to bypass local compliance and target global Tier 3-4 markets (India, Brazil, Indonesia)
- Result: Vietnam is simultaneously a major global game exporter and a heavily gated domestic market
visual:
Multi-zone overview layout (no single large image needed).
Top strip: 5-6 KPI chips showing ecosystem scale (studios, publishers, revenue, downloads, CAGR).
Middle: two side-by-side panels — left panel shows the 3-step capability arc (outsourcing -> mobile publishing -> premium/Web3); right panel shows the policy environment (2-3 bullets on dual structure: support + control).
Bottom callout bar: the structural paradox — export-first market, 94.47% downloads are international, domestic market is the gated exception not the rule.
takeaway: Vietnam's gaming supply side has matured on two fronts simultaneously: a growing base of studios delivering at global publishing scale, and a policy environment that actively promotes domestic capability while keeping the domestic market tightly gated. Slides 22-26 unpack each dimension.
notes: Source: Vietnam Game Production Ecosystem_ Evolution.docx; Vietnamese Game Development Government Support.docx

title: SUPPORT FOR DOMESTIC GAME DEVELOPMENT: DIRECT & INDIRECT
purpose: Show how the Vietnamese state has built a three-layer enabling stack for domestic studios — a promotional ecosystem, a fiscal concession architecture, and a formal talent pipeline.
evidence:
Use only the government-support research doc. Organize around three layers:

Promotional Ecosystem:
- GameVerse (state expo): 20K visitors (2023) -> 50K (2026); Google, Meta, Roblox, Riot as partners
- GameHub incubator: direct investment for selected local studios
- Vietnam Game Alliance (2021): VNG, Garena, VTC, VCCorp, Viettel

Fiscal Concessions:
- 10% CIT for 15 years, 4-year full exemption + 9 years at 50%
- Full VAT exemption, 200% R&D super-deduction, SME startup tax holidays
- SCT deferral (2023): industry successfully lobbied against luxury tax on games

Talent Pipeline:
- PTIT (2024): first public Game Design degree, Game Tech Lab with FALCON
- UIT co-training with VNGGames + Roblox Global
- RMIT, FPT Polytechnic, SAMA

Image: GameVerse 2026 exhibition floor (official photo from Vietnam Game Alliance / MIC).
visual: Image placeholder left (GameVerse 2026). Right panel: three stacked layers with red left-border labels — Promotional Ecosystem, Fiscal Concessions, Talent Pipeline — each with 2-3 compact bullets.
takeaway: Beyond regulation, Vietnam has built a three-layer enabling stack for domestic studios: a visible promotional ecosystem, a generous fiscal architecture, and a formal game-development talent pipeline now entering its first generation.
notes: Source: Vietnamese Game Development Government Support.docx

subsection: Quality of Made in VN Games / Capability of VN Studios
title: CAPABILITY EVOLUTION: FROM OUTSOURCING TO GLOBAL PUBLISHING
purpose: Show how Vietnam's production capability evolved across three distinct eras — from AAA art outsourcing to mobile self-publishing at scale to credible premium PC and Web3 development.
evidence:
Use only the production-ecosystem research doc. Three eras:

Era 1 (2000-2011): Glass Egg (Call of Duty, Spider-Man, Forza) + Gameloft (1,200 staff). Built the foundational talent base.

Era 2 (2012-2019): Flappy Bird -> Amanotes, ABI/Onesoft, Zitga. Scaled high-volume mobile publishing.

Era 3 (2020-2026): Sky Mavis ($4.1B Axie Infinity + Ronin), Hoa (3 Webby Awards), Than Trung (91% Steam), God of Weapons (100K copies/2 weeks).

KPIs: 27,388 new titles (2025), 8.5B Google Play downloads, 94.47% export share.

Image: Hoa game art (Skrollcat Studio).
visual: Timeline left: 3 stacked era cards with red left-border (years + label + anchor studios + 1-line copy + connector between eras). Right: image placeholder for Hoa key art. Bottom: 3 KPI chips.
takeaway: Vietnam's studio capability grew across three eras — from executing others' visions to shipping globally competitive products — but output remains overwhelmingly export-oriented.
notes: Source: Vietnam Game Production Ecosystem_ Evolution.docx

subsection: Quality of Made in VN Games / Capability of VN Studios
title: MADE IN VIETNAM: STUDIO PROOF POINTS
purpose: Provide concrete evidence of current Vietnamese studio capability through four flagship studios — each representing a different achievement tier.
evidence:
Use only the production-ecosystem research doc. Four studios with specific numbers:

Amanotes: Magic Tiles 3 (1B+ downloads), first SEA publisher in 3B-download club (2023), 100M+ MAUs.
Sky Mavis: Axie Infinity $4.1B peak market cap, Ronin sidechain, recovered from $625M exploit.
Skrollcat: Hoa (2021) — 3 Webby Awards, 91K+ Steam copies, ~$938K. Hoa 2 announced 2026.
DUT Studio: Than Trung (2022) — 3-person team, 91% Very Positive Steam, 89K units, $594K+.

Caveat: output overwhelmingly export-oriented; senior talent scarce.
Images: one game screenshot per studio card.
visual: 2x2 grid of studio cards. Each card: image placeholder left (game screenshot) + studio name + game title + headline stat (big red number) + 2-line description. Bottom: 1-line caveat in grey italic.
takeaway: Vietnamese studios now ship globally competitive products across mobile publishing scale, blockchain infrastructure, award-winning indie, and culturally grounded narrative titles — though most are built for export markets first.
notes: Source: Vietnam Game Production Ecosystem_ Evolution.docx
